one_line_takeaway: A simulation-only system that pairs SLM surprisal-based prompt compression plus lightweight permutation encryption (PCAE) with a group-sampling, KL-regularized PPO controller (GPPO) to send LLM prompts to the cloud both compactly and covertly, cutting preprocessing latency ~10^5x and covert-transmission latency up to 38.6%.
key_finding: PCAE compresses prompts by over 40% (concrete example: a 1485-token 3GPP prompt reduced to 891 tokens) while keeping BERTScore fidelity high (~0.82-0.89, rising with compression ratio and model size), and it does so with roughly five orders of magnitude lower preprocessing latency than the JPPO baseline -- a constant ~0.089 s vs JPPO's >10 s at aggressive compression. On the transmission side, GPPO yields the lowest latency among RL controllers, cutting covert-transmission latency by up to 38.6% (at Fmin=0.82) versus prior RL strategies, with a 40% reduction over Pure PPO and 9.8% over GRPO at Fmin=0.85, and ~14%/35% higher accumulated reward than GRPO/Pure PPO. GPPO also consistently keeps the warden detection error above the covertness threshold where baselines occasionally violate it.
core_contribution: The paper proposes a system for covert (undetectable) transmission of user prompts to cloud-hosted LLMs over wireless channels, combining content-level protection with physical-layer stealth. Its two named, self-authored artifacts are: (1) PCAE (Prompt Compression And Encryption), which uses a locally deployed small language model (SLM) to score tokens by surprisal, keeps the most informative tokens (with a head/tail preservation heuristic), then applies a lightweight offset+permutation token-level encryption; and (2) GPPO (Group-based Proximal Policy Optimization), a deep-RL controller that jointly tunes the prompt compression ratio and transmit power to minimize end-to-end latency subject to LLM response-fidelity and covertness constraints. The authors frame this as a latency-minimization problem under a fidelity constraint and a warden-detection-error (covertness) constraint, and claim to be the first to combine prompt encryption/compression with covert wireless transmission for LLM services. Note: JPPO, EmojiCrypt, SecPE, PromSec, and GRPO are cited prior work (JPPO and GRPO are used as baselines), NOT contributions of this paper; GPPO is presented as a novel variant of GRPO that re-adds a critic network.
threat_model: A user (Alice) sends prompts to a cloud LLM (Bob) over an open wireless channel. Two adversarial concerns: (a) content confidentiality -- an eavesdropper could intercept and read semantically sensitive prompts (financial, medical, legal); addressed by token-level encryption. (b) covertness/detection avoidance -- a passive warden (Willie) performs binary hypothesis testing on received signal energy (average power over T samples vs a threshold) to detect whether Alice is transmitting at all. Willie has imperfect noise-power knowledge, modeled as a log-uniform random variance over [sigma^2/mu, sigma^2*mu]. Covertness requires Willie's minimum total detection error (false-alarm + missed-detection) to stay above 1-epsilon (epsilon=0.05). The paper explicitly scopes only the uplink (Alice->Bob); Bob's downlink response is assumed lower-risk and out of scope. Key exchange for the shared secret (offset vector + permutation map) is assumed to occur out-of-band via standard protocols.
limitations: Entirely simulation-based; no real wireless hardware or over-the-air covertness measurement. The encryption is a lightweight offset+permutation scheme keyed by a pre-shared secret; the paper gives no formal cryptographic security analysis, and it even cites work [35] on the known weaknesses of permutation-only schemes -- so confidentiality guarantees are weak and rest on secure key exchange that is assumed, not provided. The threat model is a single passive energy-detector warden with a specific log-uniform noise-uncertainty assumption; smarter or feature-based (e.g., CSI) detectors are discussed as motivation but not evaluated against. Only uplink is protected; the downlink response is assumed low-risk without analysis. Fidelity is a BERTScore proxy rather than task accuracy, evaluated on a single summarization dataset (MeetingBank-QA). Table I lists discount factor gamma=0 (myopic reward), which is unusual and unexplained. Several quantitative results are read from figures (Figs. 5-10) rather than tables, and the compression/encryption 'security' is demonstrated qualitatively (an unreadable token stream) rather than via an attack-based evaluation.

datasets_used: MeetingBank-QA-Summary (real-world meeting transcripts, long-document summarization benchmark)
Nokia 3GPP Release 20 blog post (single-example visualization/demo of compression+encryption)
quantitative_results: Prompt token reduction over 40%; concrete example 1485 -> 891 tokens on a 3GPP prompt
PCAE preprocessing latency ~0.089 s (constant across compression ratios) vs JPPO baseline >10 s -- reduction of ~5 orders of magnitude (10^5)
LLM response fidelity Ft (BERTScore) ranges ~0.82-0.89 across compression ratios and model sizes; larger models (32B) higher than 14B/7B
GPPO reduces covert transmission latency by up to 38.6% at Fmin=0.82 vs existing RL strategies
At Fmin=0.85: GPPO 40% lower latency than Pure PPO and 9.8% lower than GRPO
GPPO accumulated reward ~14% higher than GRPO and ~35% higher than Pure PPO
Under high covertness demand (1-epsilon=0.97), Pure PPO latency gap exceeds 25%; GPPO latency amplitude stays within 2.1%
Fidelity/latency trade-off: latency increments of +3.8%/+8.8%/+9.8%/+12.0% as Fmin rises 0.82->0.86
baselines_compared: JPPO [41] (DRL-based prompt compression) -- compared for fidelity and compression compute time
Pure PPO [26] (single-action-per-state standard PPO) -- covert transmission controller baseline
GRPO [36] (group relative policy optimization, critic-free) -- covert transmission controller baseline
